# Enable transparent proxy mode on VMs

This topic describes how to use transparent proxy mode in your service mesh. Transparent proxy allows applications to communicate through the service mesh without modifying their configurations. Transparent proxy also hardens application security by preventing direct inbound connections that bypass the mesh. Refer to [Transparent proxy overview](/consul/docs/connect/proxy/transparent-proxy) for additional information.

## Requirements

Your network must meet the following environment and software requirements to use transparent proxy.

* A running Consul 1.11.1+ datacenter with at least two Linux client nodes.
* A dedicated user to run Consul. Consul uses the user ID to exclude Consul-related traffic from transparent proxy. In this page we use the `consul` Linux user.
* [DNS forwarding](/consul/docs/manage/dns/forwarding) enabled on the Consul client nodes.
* Envoy installed on the Consul client nodes. Check [Envoy proxy configuration reference](/consul/docs/reference/proxy/envoy#envoy-and-consul-client-agent) for information over supported versions.
* A dedicated user to run Envoy. Consul uses the user ID to exclude proxy-related traffic from transparent proxy. In this page we use the `envoy` Linux user.

## Manual configuration

To manually configure Consul service mesh to use transparent proxies follow these steps:

1. [Register the service](#register-the-service)
1. [Redirect traffic to proxies](#redirect-traffic-to-proxies)
1. [Connect services](#connect-services)

### Register the service

Configure a service to use transparent proxy mode.

<CodeBlockConfig highlight="3-9">

```json
{
 "service": {
   "connect": {
     "sidecar_service": {
       "proxy": {
         "mode": "transparent"
       }
     }
   },
   "name": "${service_name}",
   "port": ${service_port}
 }
}
```
</CodeBlockConfig>

Register the service in Consul, refer to [Register services and health checks](/consul/docs/register/service/vm) for more information.

Consul assigns a virtual IP in the `240.0.0.0/4` range to a service with transparent proxy enabled. 
This allows downstream services to connect to upstream services using a unique IP assigned by the service mesh. 

### Redirect traffic to proxies

Use the consul [`connect redirect-traffic`](/consul/commands/connect/redirect-traffic) command to enable transparent proxy on the VM for the named service.

The command includes the user ID for `consul` and `envoy` in order for transparent proxy to exclude traffic from Consul and Envoy from redirection.

```shell-session
$ consul connect redirect-traffic \
   -proxy-id="${service_name}-sidecar-proxy" \
   -proxy-uid="$(id --user envoy)" \
   -exclude-uid="$(id --user consul)"
```

<Warning>
  The command adds iptables rules that will deny existing inbound traffic unless you have specified exclusions. As a result, any connection to the VM not related to Consul and Envoy will be disconnected, including SSH.
</Warning>

To exclude inbound or outbound traffic from specific ports or IP addresses, append additional arguments specifying exclusions. 

For example, you may need to exclude inbound traffic to the SSH port for login:

```shell-session
$ consul connect redirect-traffic \
   -proxy-id="${service_name}-sidecar-proxy" \
   -proxy-uid="$(id --user envoy)" \
   -exclude-uid="$(id --user consul)" \
   -exclude-inbound-port=22
```

If you have a mesh configuration entry defined with [`TransparentProxy.MeshDestinationsOnly=true`](/consul/docs/reference/config-entry/mesh#mesh-destinations-only), this setting will deny all inbound and outbound requests to the VM once you redirect traffic. Make sure you install the application and its dependencies before redirecting traffic since transparent proxy will deny requests to download packages by default.

### Connect services

If ACLs are enabled, Consul service mesh denies all traffic between services by default.

Consul configures the proxies to route traffic to the appropriate upstream services based on [service intentions](/consul/docs/reference/config-entry/service-intentions).

This example shows an intention that allows traffic between service `web` and service `api`.

```hcl
Kind = "service-intentions"
Name = "api"
Sources = [
  {
    Name   = "web"
    Action = "allow"
    Namespace  = "default"
    Partition  = "default"  
  }
]
```

Verify you can resolve the `api` service from the `web` service client node. 

Consul DNS resolves the in-mesh virtual IP of an upstream service using a DNS hostname in the format of `${service_name}.virtual.consul`

<CodeBlockConfig hideClipboard>

```shell-session
$ nslookup api.virtual.consul
Server:         127.0.0.53
Address:        127.0.0.53#53
 
Non-authoritative answer:
Name:   api.virtual.consul
Address: 240.0.0.2
```

</CodeBlockConfig>

Verify you can reach the `api` service from the `web` service client node.

<CodeBlockConfig hideClipboard>

```shell-session
$ curl --head http://api.virtual.consul
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Date: Wed, 25 Mar 2026 13:37:36 GMT
Content-Length: 234
Content-Type: text/plain; charset=utf-8
Connection: close
```

</CodeBlockConfig>

## Automatic configuration

The process for enabling transparent proxy is standard and requires the same steps for all services.

After testing the process manually it is recommended you configure your nodes to automatically setup the sidecar proxy when you start the service.

Possible ways to automate the transparent proxy configuration are:

- [Setup up a systemd unit file](#systemd-unit-file)
- [Use Terraform](#terraform)

### Systemd unit file

If you use systemd to start Consul service mesh's sidecar proxy, you can add two scripts to the unit file to:

- Enable traffic redirection before you start the proxy
- Clean up iptables rules after you stop the proxy

<CodeBlockConfig highlight="14,16">

```shell-session
[Unit]
Description=Consul Envoy
After=syslog.target network.target consul.service
Wants=consul.service

ConditionFileIsExecutable=<script for redirecting traffic>
ConditionFileIsExecutable=<script for cleaning up iptables>

[Service]
Type=simple
User=envoy
Group=envoy
EnvironmentFile=/etc/consul.d/consul.env
ExecStartPre=+<script for redirecting traffic> ${service_name}
ExecStart=/usr/bin/consul connect envoy -sidecar-for=${service_name}
ExecStopPost=+<script for cleaning up iptables>
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
WorkingDirectory=/etc/consul.d/

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

</CodeBlockConfig>

The next section contains links to examples for these two scripts.

### Terraform

You can use Terraform to deploy Consul client nodes with services installed and configured to be part of Consul service mesh.

Use the [Terraform module for Consul transparent proxy](https://github.com/hashicorp/terraform-template-consul-tproxy) as a template to create you own deployment.

The module contains:

- An [example systemd unit](https://github.com/hashicorp/terraform-template-consul-tproxy/blob/main/templates/scripts/setup.sh) file.
- An [example script for redirecting the traffic](https://github.com/hashicorp/terraform-template-consul-tproxy/blob/main/templates/scripts/consul-tproxy-redirect.sh) before you start the sidecar proxy.
- An [example script for cleaning up iptables rules](https://github.com/hashicorp/terraform-template-consul-tproxy/blob/main/templates/scripts/consul-tproxy-cleanup.sh) after you stop the proxy.
